summary: I was sent to the Tule Lake War Relocation Center when I was about 19 years old, right after the war started. Tule Lake became a segregation camp when the so-called no-no boys and pro-Japanese people were sent there. My mother refused to leave Tule Lake because she wanted to go back home, but the War Relocation Authority had not allowed families to return yet. I lived in Block 45, and the camp was huge with many wards and blocks, each with its own facilities. Life there was hard, but we made friends and tried to keep to ourselves despite the tensions caused by the no-no boys.

The War Relocation Authority was the government agency responsible for placing us in camps like Tule Lake. They controlled when families could return home, and during the war, they questioned our loyalty with that infamous questionnaire. My mother wanted to go back home, but the WRA had not authorized it yet. They also managed the segregation of no-no boys into Tule Lake, which caused a lot of turmoil among the internees. The WRA's actions deeply affected our lives and caused much resentment.

I was born and raised on a fruit farm in Loomis, California, which was about an hour north of Sacramento. Growing up there, I started kindergarten at four and went through grammar school before being bused to Auburn for high school. My family worked hard on the farm, and I had to start helping at age five. Loomis was my childhood home before the war uprooted us and sent us to Tule Lake.

Sacramento was the nearest big city to my hometown Loomis, about an hour's drive south. It was part of the region where I grew up and went to school before the war. The area around Sacramento was where many Japanese American families lived and worked on farms before being relocated. Sacramento represented the life we had before everything changed with the war and forced removal.

For high school, I was bused from Loomis to Auburn, California, where I attended for four years. Auburn also had a junior college, which I considered attending, but my interest was in fashion design, so I looked toward bigger cities instead. Auburn was an important part of my education and growing up before the war disrupted everything. It was a small town compared to where I wanted to go next.

I wanted to attend UC Berkeley to study fashion design, but the costs and rampant discrimination in the area made it difficult. Jobs for Japanese Americans were limited, and it was hard to find opportunities. Despite my desire, I couldn't afford to go or find work easily. Berkeley represented a dream that was out of reach during those tough times.

I encountered a Navy family who rented the house where I worked, and they were very anti-Japanese, likely because of the attack on Pearl Harbor. The elderly couple and their young son made those times difficult for me. The Navy, as an institution, was closely tied to the war against Japan, and that affected how Japanese Americans like me were treated. Their hostility was a reminder of the prejudice we faced even outside the camps.

The attack on Pearl Harbor was the event that changed everything for Japanese Americans like me. After December 7, 1941, suspicion and hatred grew, and we were treated as enemy aliens despite being American citizens. It was because of Pearl Harbor that the government issued Executive Order 9066 and started the forced relocation. The attack made life very hard, with discrimination and restrictions everywhere.

San Francisco was the city where I wanted to continue my studies and eventually worked for a time. However, getting there was difficult because I was not allowed to buy a train ticket due to my Japanese heritage. Friends helped me find a way around the restrictions, and I managed to cross into the city despite the Bay Bridge checkpoints. San Francisco was a big city with crime and challenges, but I was grateful to be there to pursue my goals.

The Bay Bridge was a physical barrier for Japanese Americans like me trying to get into San Francisco during the war. Authorities would stop us and not allow us to cross because we were considered enemy aliens. I had to find alternate routes with friends to avoid crossing the bridge checkpoints. The Bay Bridge symbolized the restrictions and segregation we faced even outside the camps.

Virginia was a tall Caucasian girl in my class before the war who made a striking comment about government power. During a discussion about the possibility of Japanese Americans being incarcerated if war broke out, she said, 'The government can do anything they want to.' That statement stuck with me and proved true when we were forcibly relocated. Virginia's words were a sobering reminder of the harsh reality we faced.

The Issei were the first-generation Japanese immigrants, including my parents. During the loyalty questionnaire, many Issei parents advised their sons to answer no to the loyalty questions, which caused a lot of turmoil. They were caught between their heritage and their lives in America, and their responses influenced the treatment we received in camps like Tule Lake. The Issei's position was difficult and deeply affected our family.

The no-no boys were those who answered no to the loyalty questionnaire, and many were sent to Tule Lake, which became a segregation camp for them. They were often seen as pro-Japanese and caused tension within the camp community. I was in Tule Lake when the no-no boys arrived, and although they created dissension, they didn't bother me personally. Their presence marked a difficult chapter in our camp experience.

entity: Block 45, Tule Lake War Relocation Center
summary: I lived in Block 45 at Tule Lake, which was one of many blocks in the camp, each with its own kitchen and bathrooms. The camp was divided into wards and blocks with firebreaks between them, so we had to walk a lot to get around. Block 45 was my home during the war, and despite the hardships, I had friends nearby and tried to maintain some normalcy. It was a tough place, but it was where we lived for the duration.

I dreamed of going to Chicago to pursue fashion design because it was a major center for the industry. When I first got into camp, I told my mother I wanted to go there, but she firmly refused to let me leave. Chicago represented opportunity and a fresh start, but I was stuck in Tule Lake instead. It was a hope that never materialized during those years.

New York City was another place I wanted to go for fashion design, as it was a fashion hub. Like Chicago, I asked my mother if I could go, but she said no and would not support me financially. New York symbolized the kind of life and career I aspired to but could not pursue because of family and wartime restrictions. It remained a distant dream while I was confined to Tule Lake.

Japan was the country we were accused of being loyal to during the war, which caused so much pain and confusion. I am angry at Japan for creating the war that led to our incarceration and the loyalty questionnaire. Many families had relatives in Japan, and the war made life hard for them, especially with food shortages. Japan's actions affected us deeply, even though we were American born and raised.

I was born and raised in the United States and always considered myself American. Yet during the war, the government treated me and my family as enemy aliens because of our Japanese ancestry. The U.S. government forced us into camps, questioned our loyalty, and restricted our freedoms. Despite this, I still think of America as my home, though I carry resentment for how we were treated.

I read the book The Train to Crystal City, which opened my eyes to the government's plan for enemy exchanges during the war. The book explained how families like mine in Tule Lake could be sent to Crystal City Internment Camp for exchange with Japanese nationals held in the U.S. It revealed the harsh realities and political motives behind the internment, deepening my understanding of what we endured.

Earl Warren was a figure I learned about who hated Japanese Americans during the war. Although I never met him, I felt his hatred through the policies and attitudes that affected us. He once lived in Alameda, California, and his prejudice contributed to the suffering we endured. His legacy is a reminder of the deep-seated racism we faced from powerful people.

Alameda is where Earl Warren once lived, a place connected to the man who played a role in the discrimination against Japanese Americans. Though I never lived there, Alameda is part of the story of wartime California and the prejudice we faced. It symbolizes the local roots of some of the hostility toward our community during the war.

Franklin D. Roosevelt was the president who signed Executive Order 9066, which authorized the forced removal and incarceration of Japanese Americans. I believe he hated us and was responsible for much of the suffering we endured. The book I read suggested he had reasons related to enemy exchanges with Japan and Germany, but his actions caused great pain. Roosevelt's decisions shaped the course of our lives during the war.

Executive Order 9066 was the government order that led to our forced removal from our homes and placement in camps like Tule Lake. Signed by President Roosevelt after Pearl Harbor, it gave the military authority to exclude people from certain areas. This order uprooted our lives and questioned our loyalty, even though we were American citizens. It was the legal basis for the injustice we suffered during the war.

Crystal City Internment Camp was a place where families from Tule Lake and other camps were sent for enemy exchange during the war. The government used it to swap Japanese nationals held here with Americans held in Japan and Germany. I learned about it from the book The Train to Crystal City, which explained how it was part of a larger political plan. It was a grim reminder of how families were separated and moved around like pawns.

Germany was one of the Axis powers during World War II, alongside Japan, and part of the enemy exchange program the U.S. government planned. Some Americans held in Germany were to be exchanged for Japanese nationals here in the United States. This connection was part of the reason for camps like Crystal City and the forced relocations. Germany's role in the war indirectly affected Japanese American families like mine.
